label: vulnerable

source.description: User-controlled string bound from the HTTP query parameter via @RequestParam in the controller layer. Spring performs no sanitization during binding.

propagation.description: filename is forwarded unchanged to FileService.getFileContent() — no validation at the controller-to-service boundary.
FileService.getFileContent() passes filename directly to FileUtil.readFile() without any sanitization — no validation at the service-to-util boundary.
Paths.get(BASE_DIR, filename) appends the tainted string. '..' sequences are preserved literally and not normalized.

sink.description: Files.readAllBytes() reads the file at the unvalidated path. An attacker can supply '../../etc/passwd' to read arbitrary files accessible to the process.

missing_controls: validation at service or util boundary before Paths.get()
expected_findings.risk_reasoning: The filename value propagates through three layers (controller → service → util) without any validation. It enters Paths.get() and then Files.readAllBytes() in the util layer. '..' traversal sequences are not stripped at any layer, enabling arbitrary file read.
expected_findings.remediation_hint: In FileUtil.readFile(), replace Paths.get(BASE_DIR, filename) with BASE_DIR_PATH.resolve(filename).normalize(), then call toRealPath() and assert startsWith(BASE_DIR_PATH.toRealPath()) before reading.

recall_anchor.description: A correct system must identify the sink in util/FileUtil.java. Source identification in controller/FileController.java is additional credit.

notes: This case tests cross-file inter-procedural taint tracing across Java package layers: controller (source) → service (passthrough) → util (sink). Each layer boundary is an opportunity for a system to lose track of the taint.
